Twigs

Twigs are the latest growth of the tree, the small branches that grow leaves, flowers and fruit. This is where most of the tree’s geometric detail resides, which is why The Grove uses twig instances to keep the tree (relatively) lightweight.


The Grove’s twigs are objects created with regular polygonal geometry, and you can use your own geometry, like when you’re creating game trees.


Twig duplication

The tree geometry has thousands of little triangles alongside and at the end of branches. To Scatter points and to instance twig models, the Build node creates primitive groups gr_twig_long, gr_twig_short, gr_twig_upward and gr_twig_dead. The build node also creates orientation quaternions in the orient attribute. Or create your own orientations using the normal direction of the twig triangles.


Match the preset to the twig

This is a very important concept to grasp, which will save you a lot of time, so read this carefully.


The Grove comes with dozens of presets that capture a wide variety of trees. The Grove also offers dozens of twigs, again with a good variety from trees across the world. But the presets don’t always match the twigs, and not every preset or twig has a matching bark texture.


It would be impossible to capture the growth character and a set of twigs of all trees on the planet. Actually, the goal is to reduce the number of presets even further and make it even easier to tweak the preset to get a specific look.


Luckily, there are many groups of trees that share similar growth strategies and have the same character and look to them. There is however quite some variation in the rate of growth and the size of the leaves and twigs.


There is an easy way to match the size of any preset to the size of any twig, so that you have the freedom to mix-and-match. You can use any twig on any preset, and simply scale the branches before duplicating twigs across the tree. That way the twigs keep the real world scale, and you can adjust the density of the foliage.


Now why wouldn’t you go ahead and tweak the growth parameters of the tree and create a new preset? Well, you will soon notice that all the parameters are closely influenced by every other parameter - especially when you try to make it grow smaller or bigger. This is because The Grove is a simulation with real physics. Something as simple as increasing the yearly length of growth, will also make the branch bend more, unless you also increase the thickness. Then the shade geometry will still be the same size, and it will cast less shade, which makes the tree loose less branches, again making the tree gain more weight and bend more. It’s a careful balancing act that can be very rewarding. But if there is a preset that matches the look and simply is the wrong scale, don’t go that route and just scale the tree before copying twigs.


Here’s an example where in the first image, the tree is at its original scale, using the olive twig. The second image shows the exact same tree, grown with the exact same preset, only scaled up 3x to accommodate the much larger yellow bu twig with its huge compound leaves.




#the grove/the grove in houdini/manual#
